q: Harshavardhana (c. 606-647 CE) ruled from which capital?
options: Kannauj (modern UP, he shifted from Thanesar)
explanation: Harshavardhana (c. 606-647 CE) ruled from Kannauj (modern UP). He originally belonged to Thanesar (Haryana) but shifted his capital to Kannauj after conquering it. He was the last great Hindu emperor of northern India — ruling from Punjab to Bengal, and south to the Narmada (he was stopped by Pulakeshin II of the Chalukyas).

q: Harsha's biography 'Harshacharita' was written by which author?
explanation: Banabhatta wrote the 'Harshacharita' (Biography of Harsha) — a Sanskrit prose work. Banabhatta was Harsha's court poet. He also wrote the 'Kadambari' (a Sanskrit romance, completed by his son Bhushanabhatta). The Harshacharita is one of the few biographical works in ancient Indian literature.

q: The Chinese pilgrim Hiuen Tsang (Xuanzang) visited India during whose reign?
explanation: Hiuen Tsang (Xuanzang) visited India during Harsha's reign (c. 630-645 CE). He spent ~15 years in India, studied at Nalanda University (Bihar) for 5 years, and met Harsha at Kannauj (c. 643 CE). His travelogue 'Si-yu-ki' (Records of the Western Regions) is a crucial source for post-Gupta India.

q: Harsha was defeated by which southern king at the Battle of Narmada (c. 634 CE)?
options: Pulakeshin II (Chalukya king of Badami, Karnataka)
explanation: Harsha was defeated by Pulakeshin II (Chalukya king of Badami, Karnataka) at the Battle of Narmada (c. 634 CE). Pulakeshin II's Aihole inscription (by Ravikirti, c. 634 CE) records this victory: 'Harsha's harsha (joy) melted away through fear.' The Narmada became the boundary between Harsha's empire and the Chalukya kingdom.

q: Harsha was a patron of which religions?
options: Both Buddhism and Hinduism (he was a Shaivite who later patronised Buddhism)
explanation: Harsha was a Shaivite (Hindu) who later patronised Buddhism. He organised the Kanauj Assembly (c. 643 CE) — a great Buddhist council attended by Hiuen Tsang. He also organised the Prayag Assembly (an annual charity event). He was tolerant of all religions. He wrote three Sanskrit plays: Ratnavali, Priyadarshika, and Nagananda.

q: The Chalukya dynasty of Badami ruled which region (c. 6th-8th century CE)?
options: Karnataka and parts of Andhra (Deccan, with capital at Badami)
explanation: The Chalukyas of Badami (c. 543-757 CE) ruled Karnataka and parts of Andhra (Deccan). Capital: Badami (Vatapi, Karnataka). Founder: Pulakeshin I (c. 543 CE). Greatest king: Pulakeshin II (c. 610-642 CE) — who defeated Harsha. They were Feudatories of the Kadambas initially, then became independent.

q: Pulakeshin II's Aihole inscription (c. 634 CE), which records his victory over Harsha, was composed by which poet?
options: Ravikirti (a Jain poet at Pulakeshin's court)
explanation: The Aihole inscription (c. 634 CE) was composed by Ravikirti — a Jain poet at Pulakeshin II's court. It records Pulakeshin's victory over Harsha ('Harsha's harsha melted away through fear'). It is one of the finest Sanskrit inscriptions and describes the Chalukya dynasty's achievements. The inscription is at the Meguti Jain temple at Aihole.

q: The Pallava dynasty ruled which region (c. 4th-9th century CE)?
options: Northern Tamil Nadu (Tondaimandalam, with capital at Kanchipuram)
explanation: The Pallavas (c. 4th-9th century CE) ruled northern Tamil Nadu (Tondaimandalam). Capital: Kanchipuram. Greatest kings: Mahendravarman I (c. 590-630 CE) and Narasimhavarman I (c. 630-668 CE). Narasimhavarman I defeated Pulakeshin II (c. 642 CE) and sacked Badami — ending Pulakeshin's reign. The Pallavas were known for rock-cut temples (Mahabalipuram) and Dravidian temple architecture.

q: The famous rock-cut temples at Mahabalipuram (Mamallapuram, Tamil Nadu) were built by which dynasty?
options: The Pallava dynasty (especially Narasimhavarman I and Rajasimha, c. 7th-8th century CE)
explanation: The Mahabalipuram (Mamallapuram) rock-cut temples and rathas (monolithic chariots) were built by the Pallava dynasty — especially Narasimhavarman I (c. 630-668 CE, called 'Mamalla' = great wrestler) and later Rajasimha. UNESCO World Heritage Site (1984). Features: Pancha Rathas (five chariots), Shore Temple, Descent of the Ganges (giant bas-relief).

q: The Sangam Age (c. 300 BCE - 300 CE) refers to the period of Tamil literary assemblies. Where were the Sangams held?
options: Madurai (the first two Sangams) and the third at North Madurai (all in Tamil Nadu)
explanation: The Sangam assemblies were held in Tamil Nadu: (1) First Sangam at Madurai (attended by gods and sages, no texts survive). (2) Second Sangam at Kapadapuram (only the Tolkappiyam survives). (3) Third Sangam at North Madurai (most surviving Sangam literature comes from this). The Sangams were patronised by the Pandya kings of Madurai.

q: The Sangam literature was patronised by which three dynasties (the 'Muvendar')?
options: The Cheras, Cholas, and Pandyas (the three Tamil kingdoms)
explanation: The Sangam literature was patronised by the three Tamil kingdoms (the 'Muvendar'): (1) Cheras (Kerala, capital Vanji/Karur, emblem: bow), (2) Cholas (Tamil Nadu, capital Uraiyur/Kaveripattinam, emblem: tiger), (3) Pandyas (southern Tamil Nadu, capital Madurai, emblem: fish). They ruled South India during the Sangam Age (c. 300 BCE - 300 CE).

q: The greatest Chera king of the Sangam Age, known for his generosity ('the Chera who gave without stint'), was:
options: Senguttuvan (c. 2nd century CE, also called 'Red Chera')
explanation: Senguttuvan (c. 2nd century CE) was the greatest Chera king — called 'Red Chera' (Chenguttuvan). He was known for his generosity ('the Chera who gave without stint'). He is credited with founding the Pattini cult (worship of Kannagi as the ideal wife) — after the Silappadikaram story. He crossed the Ganga (north India expedition) — described in the Silappadikaram.

q: The greatest Chola king of the Sangam Age, known for building the Kallanai dam (Grand Anicut) on the Kaveri, was:
explanation: Karikala (c. 2nd century CE) was the greatest Chola king of the Sangam Age. He built the Kallanai dam (Grand Anicut) on the Kaveri river — one of the oldest dams in the world (still in use). He won the Battle of Venni (defeating the Cheras and Pandyas). The later Cholas (9th-13th c. CE, Imperial Cholas) claimed descent from Karikala.

q: The greatest Pandya king of the Sangam Age, known for his patronage of Sangam literature, was:
options: Nedunjeliyan (c. 2nd century CE, also called 'Ariyapadai Kadantha Nedunjeliyan')
explanation: Nedunjeliyan (c. 2nd century CE) was the greatest Pandya king of the Sangam Age. He was called 'Ariyapadai Kadantha' (one who defeated the Aryans). The Sangam poems describe his court at Madurai as a great literary centre. The Pandyas patronised the Third Sangam at Madurai — where most surviving Sangam literature was compiled.

q: The Silappadikaram (one of the three Tamil epics) was written by which author?
options: Ilango Adigal (a Chera prince, c. 2nd century CE)
explanation: The Silappadikaram (The Anklet Story) was written by Ilango Adigal — a Chera prince (c. 2nd century CE). It tells the story of Kovalan and Kannagi (the ideal wife). Kannagi's husband Kovalan is unjustly executed by the Pandya king in Madurai — Kannagi tears off her breast and burns Madurai. It celebrates the Pattini (chaste wife) cult. One of the five great epics of Tamil literature.

q: The Thirukkural (a classic Tamil text of 1,330 couplets on ethics, politics, and love) was written by:
explanation: The Thirukkural was written by Thiruvalluvar (c. 1st-2nd century CE). It has 1,330 couplets (kurals) in 133 chapters, divided into three books: Aram (Virtue/Ethics), Porul (Wealth/Politics), and Inbam (Love). It is considered the 'Tamil Veda' — a universal text on ethics and morality. Translated into many languages worldwide.

q: The Manimekalai (the second Tamil epic, a sequel to the Silappadikaram) was written by:
explanation: The Manimekalai was written by Sattanar (Cattanar, c. 2nd century CE). It is the sequel to the Silappadikaram — telling the story of Manimekalai (daughter of Kovalan and the dancer Madhavi). It has strong Buddhist themes — Manimekalai becomes a Buddhist nun. One of the five great epics of Tamil literature. The Manimekalai and Silappadikaram together are called the 'Twin Epics.'

q: The Sangam literature is divided into how many anthologies (the 'Ettuthogai' or Eight Anthologies)?
explanation: The Sangam literature is divided into: (1) Ettuthogai (Eight Anthologies) — 8 collections of poems (Aham, Puram, etc.), and (2) Pattupattu (Ten Idylls) — 10 longer poems. Together they form the core of Sangam literature. The poems are classified into Aham (love, inner) and Puram (war, heroic, outer) genres.

q: The Sangam poems are classified into which two main genres?
explanation: Sangam poems are classified into: (1) Aham (love/inner world) — poems about love, romance, separation, the five landscapes (tinai — each associated with a mood). (2) Puram (war/heroic/outer world) — poems about war, kings, battles, heroism, death in battle, patronage. This Aham-Puram binary is unique to Tamil Sangam literature.

q: Bihar's connection with the Post-Gupta period is primarily through which fact?
options: Hiuen Tsang studied at Nalanda University (Bihar) for 5 years during Harsha's reign
explanation: Bihar's Post-Gupta connection: Hiuen Tsang studied at Nalanda University (Bihar) for 5 years (c. 630-645 CE) during Harsha's reign. Nalanda continued to flourish as the world's greatest Buddhist university. Harsha also patronised Nalanda. Bihar was a centre of Buddhist learning — but political power had shifted to Kannauj (UP) under Harsha.

q: After Harsha's death (647 CE), his empire collapsed. Who was the Chinese ambassador at Harsha's court who left a valuable account?
options: Wang Xuance (who was attacked and then returned with a Tibetan/Nepali army)
explanation: Wang Xuance was the Chinese ambassador at Harsha's court. After Harsha's death (647 CE), he was attacked and imprisoned by the new king (Arjuna). Wang Xuance escaped, returned with a Tibetan and Nepali army, defeated Arjuna, and brought Buddhist relics to China. His accounts provide valuable information about post-Harsha India.

q: The Chalukya dynasty is known for which architectural contribution?
options: The rock-cut temples at Badami, Aihole, and Pattadakal (Karnataka)
explanation: The Chalukyas of Badami are known for rock-cut and structural temples at Badami, Aihole, and Pattadakal (Karnataka). Pattadakal is a UNESCO World Heritage Site (1987) — with temples in both North Indian (Nagara) and South Indian (Dravida) styles. Aihole is called the 'Cradle of Indian Temple Architecture' (~125 temples).

q: The Sangam Age economy was characterized by which feature?
options: Flourishing international trade (Roman trade via Muziris/Pondicherry, spices, pearls, muslin)
explanation: The Sangam Age economy featured flourishing international trade. Roman trade was a major source of wealth — Roman gold coins have been found in South India (evidence of trade). Ports: Muziris (Kerala, modern Cranganore), Puhar/Kaveripattinam (Chola port). Exports: spices (pepper, cinnamon), pearls (from Gulf of Mannar), muslin, gems, silk. The Periplus of the Erythraean Sea (1st c. CE) records this trade.

q: Which of the following is NOT a feature of the Sangam Age society?
options: A rigid caste system like north India (Sangam society had occupational groups, not rigid varna)
explanation: The Sangam Age society did NOT have a rigid caste system like north India. Instead, it had occupational groups (arasar = rulers, vaisiyar = traders, vellalar = farmers) — based on occupation, not birth. Women had relatively high status (some women poets like Avvaiyar). The 'kings' were called 'Arasar,' traders 'Vanigar,' farmers 'Vellalar' — a more fluid social structure than the north Indian varna system.

q: The Chalukya king Pulakeshin II (c. 610-642 CE) is also known for which achievement?
options: Defeating the Pallava king Mahendravarman I and resisting Arab incursions
explanation: Pulakeshin II (c. 610-642 CE) defeated the Pallava king Mahendravarman I, extended the Chalukya empire from Gujarat to Andhra, and resisted the early Arab incursions (c. 636 CE, during the Prophet Muhammad's lifetime). His Aihole inscription (Ravikirti, 634 CE) records his victories. He was ultimately defeated and killed by Narasimhavarman I (Pallava) c. 642 CE — who sacked Badami.
